summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques the growing use of predictive risk models in criminal sentencing across 20+ US states, arguing they constitute a 'weapon of math destruction.' These models use factors like unemployment, education, neighborhood, and family criminal history to predict recidivism, effectively penalizing people for being poor rather than for their actual crimes. She argues this creates discriminatory feedback loops that compound existing racial and economic biases in the justice system, while modelers defend the practice as 'scientific.'
key_points: Risk-based sentencing models use socioeconomic factors unrelated to the actual crime committed, effectively jailing poor people longer
The models create negative feedback loops: longer sentences for 'high risk' populations make them riskier in future models
Confused causality is baked in—if police arrest certain groups more often (e.g., Stop & Frisk), the model treats them as higher risk regardless of actual behavior
Claiming to only use models for sentence mitigation still discriminates, since letting low-risk wealthy people off easier is equivalent to harsher sentences for poor minorities
Defenders calling these models 'scientific' represents a severe abuse of science and mathematics
money_quotes: The modelers defend this crap as 'scientific,' which is the worst abuse of science and mathematics imaginable.
Letting a bunch of rich white people off easier because they are not considered 'high risk' is tantamount to sentencing poor minorities more.
It is also presumably unconstitutional to jail someone longer for being poor, which is what this effectively does.
The model will impose longer sentencing on the population it considers most risky, which will in turn make them even riskier in the future.
How great would it be if we used data and models to make sure rich people went to jail just as often and for just as long as poor people for the same crime, instead of the other way around?
We could easily imagine a certain group that gets arrested more often for a given crime just because the police have that practice for whatever reason.
key_insight: Predictive sentencing models launder existing social inequalities through mathematical legitimacy, punishing poverty and race while claiming scientific objectivity.
